title: Part I
paragraphs: It was in those days when I wandered about hungry in Christiania, that strange city which no one leaves before it has set its mark upon him.

I was lying awake in my attic room and I heard a clock below strike six. It was already broad daylight, and people were beginning to move up and down the stairs. By the door, where the boards of the floor formed a tiny ledge, a small bundle of notes and papers, my writings, had been deposited. The table took up so much space that I had great difficulty in reaching round it to get into bed at night; and the only chair I possessed had to stand in a corner on one side. On the walls hung a few odds and ends—a landing-net, some book shelves, two small picture-frames. I lay and tried to sleep, and, when it was not possible, lay and listened to the sounds about me—to the city awakening.

I had been lying looking up at the white ceiling, thinking and turning over all manner of things in my mind, when I suddenly felt something stir within me—a thought gnawing its way through my brain—a quite simple idea: I had not eaten since yesterday. I sat up in bed immediately to consider the situation, and my head felt strangely light; then I lifted a hand, and the hand fell back again of its own accord. Was I actually about to die of hunger? Was I actually on the point of perishing from starvation?

I arose at last, dressed, and went out. The morning air was cool. The sun shone already over the house-roofs, and I felt better outside. It was bright and cheerful everywhere, and I thought of the newspaper office. Maybe an article of mine had been printed; something that would bring in a little money. I quickened my pace. In the meantime I took to looking about me; there were people passing up and down, early risers hurrying by on their way to work. I felt light and easy, and walked along thinking of all sorts of things. The fresh air did me good; my thoughts cleared, and I felt no longer as I had done before.

It occurred to me to go to the newspaper editor and ask for money. This plan met with my approval at once: I would go and ask right out that they should give me a little in advance. I could write a sketch for the next day. I turned and went in the direction of the newspaper office. On my way I had to pass a butcher's shop, and an extraordinary longing came over me. I looked in through the window at the rows of meat, fresh and dripping with red juice. I stood and looked for a long time, and went in and bought a piece of beefsteak. It was the cheapest they had. I held the meat in my hand until I came to a baker's shop, and there I bought a loaf. By degrees I was beginning to feel better.

When I reached the newspaper office the editor was not in, but his assistant was there and received me. He was a small man, with yellow side-whiskers, and he busied himself in looking through a heap of papers as I entered.
'Ah, it is you,' said he. 'Well, I am glad you came. Wait a moment.' And he rummaged further in his papers.
I waited. It was the article about the Crimes of Futurity, I supposed. It had been in the office for a fortnight.
'Here it is,' he said at last, dragging out a manuscript. 'You must take it back. It won't do. The chief doesn't like it. Besides, we have quite enough on hand just now of that kind.'
I took back the article without a word. I was not even annoyed. I was too weak for that. My fingers fumbled mechanically with the pages, and I went out of the office.

I went into the street, and stood there for a while. I knew not what to do. Everything seemed so indifferent to me. What should I write about? What new idea could I hit upon? My brain was a blank. I stood thinking; it struck me at last that I might write a short piece about my misery—about myself. I pulled out my note-book and wrote a few words. Then I started off walking again.

I walked quickly for a little while, and then sank back into my sluggish pace. After a couple of hours I was back in my room again, and I flung myself on the bed with my clothes on. I was very tired. There was nothing to be done; I was empty, done for. Then I fell asleep.

When I woke up again it was already past mid-day. I arose feeling hungry, but I had nothing to eat. I put my hand in my pocket and found a crust of bread that I had forgotten. I ate it up, crust and all, without any pleasure; I was too hungry for that. Then I washed my face and went out again.

The streets were now lively, many people about. I looked in at the shop windows, read the bill-posters, and tried to think of something to write about, but my head was empty. I tried everything—thinking of this and that, but always the hunger forced itself into my thoughts and distracted me. I gave up the attempt, and just wandered along.

Suddenly I perceived that I was walking more erect than usual, that my step was firmer. I seemed to feel the pressure of a hat-brim that wasn't there, and a stick that I hadn't got. I was amused at myself. I came across a man whom I knew, a journalist on one of the newspapers, and I stopped him.
'Good day,' said I.
'Good day,' said he, and looked at me suspiciously.
But I was in good humour. 'A fine day,' I remarked.
'Superb,' said he shortly, and would have gone on.
'Don't be in such a hurry,' I said. 'By the way, have you heard—have you by any chance heard if Fabian Olsen has taken a situation yet?'
'No, I haven't,' he replied, and looked at his watch. 'I must be off,' he added.
'Wait a bit,' I said. 'I wanted to ask you something. Is there anything going on that I could write up?'
'God knows,' said he. 'The Council of State meets to-morrow; or is it to-day? I don't rightly know. There is something about the harbour. Good day.'
And away he went.

I was left standing there. I was angry, not so much with him as with myself. What a way to run after a man! I walked on. There was nothing to be done. The hunger was gnawing at me still; I had eaten so little for so long that it had become a kind of habit—a fixed pain that I had learned to endure. I went into a café, ordered a cup of coffee, and sat and thought.

I had been sitting for some time when a woman came in and sat down opposite me. She was young, and quietly dressed. I did not pay any particular attention to her at first; but after a little while I raised my eyes and looked at her, and she met my glance for a moment, then looked down again. She had a sweet, serious face, with large dark eyes.
I paid for my coffee, and went out.
That evening I ate a piece of bread and drank some water, and went early to bed. I was so tired that I fell asleep at once.

title: Part II
paragraphs: Some days had now passed. I had written several articles, but none had been accepted. My landlady was threatening to turn me out. I had no money at all.

I had been down in the town. It was towards evening; the streets were beginning to empty, and the shop-windows to light up. I walked along mechanically, thinking of my manuscript. I had written a short piece, a little dramatic scene, and was going to offer it to the editor of Morgenbladet. I was hungry; I had only had a little bread and water all day.
As I turned a corner I came near running into a man. It was Queeny, a newspaper man whom I knew slightly.
'Hallo!' he said, stopping. 'You look pale. What is the matter?'
'Oh, nothing,' I said. 'I have not been very well.'
'Come and have a drink,' he said.
I hesitated. I had no money, and I could not let him pay for me.
'I can't,' I said; 'I have some work to do.'
'Nonsense,' he said, and took my arm. 'Come on.'
We went into a café, and he ordered two beers.
'Well,' he said, as we sat down, 'how are things?'
'Not so bad,' I answered.
'Got plenty of work?'
'Yes—fairly,' I lied.
He talked about one thing and another, and I listened. I was very hungry, and the smell of food from the kitchen made my head swim. I drank my beer slowly.
When we came out he shook my hand.
'Look me up if you are in difficulties,' he said.
I thanked him, and we parted.

I went on home. On the way I stopped before a provision shop and stood looking at the food in the window. There was a whole cooked chicken hanging there, and some rolls of bread, and sausage. I stood looking at it for a long time. Then I went on.
When I came home I found a note from my landlady under the door. She wanted her money, or I must go. She was sorry, but she could not wait any longer.

I sat on the edge of my bed and thought it over. I must try to get some money somehow. I thought of going to the editor of Morgenbladet, but it was too late to go there that evening. I would go in the morning.
I lay down on my bed. I was very tired, and presently fell asleep.
Next morning I went to the editor. He received me kindly enough, and read my manuscript. But he shook his head.
'This is not quite the thing for us,' he said. 'It is too—how shall I put it?—too introspective. We need something more concrete, more—'
'I understand,' I said.
'Come back in a day or two with something else,' he said, 'and we will see.'
I went out. I was very low. I walked slowly down the street, thinking what I should do. I had no money, no food. I was getting very weak. I stood still for a moment and leaned against a wall.
Then I thought of a man I knew, a tradesman named Christie. I had done him a service once, a long time ago. Perhaps he would lend me a few shillings. I set off to find him.
He was in his shop when I came. He was busy with a customer, and I waited. When the customer had gone he came over to me.
'Well,' he said, 'what can I do for you?'
'I wanted to ask a favour,' I said. 'I am in some temporary difficulty—'
'I am sorry,' he interrupted, 'but I have strict rules. I never lend money. It always causes trouble. You understand.'
'Of course,' I said, and went out.
I felt humiliated. I walked aimlessly through the streets. At last I sat down on a bench in the park. The sun was warm, and I felt sleepy. I dozed a little.
When I woke up I felt better. The sun was lower now, and the shadows were long. I arose and went towards the harbour. I liked to look at the ships.
There were several vessels in the harbour, and I stood on the quay looking at them. A sailor came and stood beside me.
'Fine evening,' he said.
'Yes,' I answered.
We talked for a little while. He was from one of the ships, going south the next day. I wished I could go with him.
I walked back through the town. I was very hungry now. I passed a baker's shop, and the smell of fresh bread was almost more than I could bear. I went on.
That evening I went to bed without supper. I lay awake a long time thinking. At last I fell asleep.
I dreamed of food—great tables spread with all manner of good things to eat. I woke up in the middle of the night, very hungry, and could not get to sleep again for a long time.
In the morning I lay still for a while after waking. I felt very weak. At last I got up and dressed. I had nothing to eat. I put my hands in my pockets—nothing. I looked about the room—nothing.
I went out. The morning air was cold, and it made me feel dizzy. I steadied myself against the wall for a moment, then went on.

I walked down to the quay again. The ships were still there. I sat on a bollard and looked at them. After a while a ship's cook came ashore and went into a shop near by. When he came out I spoke to him.
'Could you give me a bit of bread?' I said.
He looked at me.
'Come aboard,' he said.
I went with him aboard the ship, and he gave me bread and a piece of salt fish and a cup of coffee. I ate it all. I felt better.
When I thanked him and was going he said: 'Come back if you are hungry again.'

I went ashore feeling somewhat stronger. The sun was up now and the day had grown warm. I went back to my room and tried to write. I wrote a few lines, but they were poor. My head was not clear enough.
I lay down on the bed and thought. I thought about a girl I had seen in the café—the girl with the dark eyes. I wondered who she was. I thought about her for a long time.
Then I fell asleep again.

title: Part III
paragraphs: Things were growing worse with me. I had been turned out of my room and was sleeping in a doorway. It was now some weeks since I had had a proper meal.

I had found a little shelter in a doorway in Tomtegaden, not far from the quay. It was not comfortable, but it was better than nothing. I would curl up in the corner and try to sleep, and sometimes I did sleep, but more often I lay awake, thinking.

One night I lay there thinking about a piece I wanted to write. It was to be about a crime—not an ordinary crime, but something subtle and psychological. I thought about it for a long time, and presently I began to feel excited. The idea seemed to me excellent. I sat up and found a pencil and some paper in my pocket, and began to write in the dark. I could not see what I was writing, but I went on.
In the morning I looked at what I had written. It was nearly illegible, but I could make out enough to see that it was good. I was pleased. I felt stronger, more alive.
I went to a café and ordered a cup of coffee. I had a few øre that I had found the day before. I sat and worked at my manuscript, and by noon I had a fair copy done.
I took it to the newspaper office. The editor was in. He read my piece, and I could see from his face that he liked it.
'This is better,' he said. 'Yes, this we can use. Come back on Friday and we will have your money ready.'
I went out with a lighter heart. Friday was three days away. I must manage somehow until then.
I went to the harbour and spoke again to the cook of the ship. He gave me food, as before, and was kind. I came every day that week. He never asked any questions.

On Friday I went to the newspaper office and got my money. It was not much—only a few kroner—but it seemed like a fortune. I went straight to a restaurant and had a meal, a real meal, with soup and meat and bread. I ate slowly, and felt it doing me good.
After eating I went to look for a room. I found one, a small place near the railway station, and paid a week in advance. It had a bed and a table and one chair. It was all I needed.
I stayed in my room that afternoon and wrote. Ideas came more easily now. I wrote steadily until evening, and what I wrote seemed to me to be good.
But in a few days the money was gone. I had spent it on food and rent, and now there was nothing again. I began to feel the old weakness returning.

One evening, walking through the town, I saw the girl again—the girl with the dark eyes from the café. She was walking with an older woman, probably her mother. I followed them a little way, not knowing why. She was very graceful in her walk.
Then she turned and saw me. She looked at me for a moment with her large dark eyes, and there was something in her look—not displeasure, but something questioning. Then she turned away.
I stood still and watched them go. Then I went on my way.
I tried again with the newspaper. I wrote several pieces, but the editor was less encouraging this time.
'This is not quite right,' he said. 'You must try again.'
I tried again. Days passed. I grew weaker. I began to lose track of time. Sometimes I did not know what day it was.
One afternoon I sat on a bench in the park. A man sat down beside me—an old man with a grey beard.
'Fine day,' he said.
'Yes,' I answered.
We sat in silence for a while. Then he asked me something, and I answered, and we talked a little. He told me he had been a sailor in his youth. He seemed a kind old man.
When he got up to go he put his hand in his pocket and took out a small coin.
'Here,' he said, 'take this. I can see you are having a hard time.'
I took the coin. I was too weak to feel shame.
'Thank you,' I said.
He went away. I sat looking at the coin in my hand. It was not much, but I could buy bread.
I got up and went to a baker's shop and bought a roll. I ate it on the street, walking along slowly.

The days went on. Sometimes I had food, sometimes nothing. I wrote when I could, but my work was getting worse. My head would not clear. I began to have strange thoughts, to see things that were not there, to hear voices.
One day I met the girl with the dark eyes on the street. She was alone this time. I stopped.
'I beg your pardon,' I said, 'but I have seen you before—in a café.'
She stopped and looked at me. Her eyes were very beautiful.
'Yes,' she said, 'I think I have seen you too.'
'My name is—' I started to say, but then stopped. What was my name to her?
She waited.
'I wanted to say,' I began again, 'that you have very beautiful eyes.'
She looked surprised, then smiled slightly.
'Thank you,' she said, and walked on.
I stood and watched her go. I felt suddenly happy, lighter than I had felt in weeks.
But the feeling did not last. That night I lay in my doorway again—I had lost my room—and the hunger came back worse than ever.

I lay looking up at the sky. The stars were out. I thought about the girl. I gave her a name: Ylajali. I did not know why—it was just a name that came to me. Ylajali. I said it over and over to myself in the dark.

title: Part IV
paragraphs: Summer had passed and it was autumn. The days were shorter now, and there was a chill in the air at night. I was still in Christiania, still hungry, still writing.

I had had a few more pieces printed, and for a week or two things had been somewhat better. But now I was at the bottom again. I had written nothing for several days that I could bring myself to offer to the newspapers.

One morning I awoke in my old shelter in the doorway with a clear head and a strange feeling of energy. It was early, and the streets were quiet. I sat up and looked about me. The sky was grey, and there was a smell of rain in the air.

I took out my pencil and notebook and began to write. I wrote for two hours without stopping. What I wrote was different from anything I had written before—stranger, more intense. When I stopped at last I felt exhausted but satisfied.
I read it over. It was good. I knew it was good. I got up and went to the newspaper office.
The editor was not in, but his assistant received me. I gave him the manuscript. He glanced over it.
'Come back this afternoon,' he said.
I went out and walked about until afternoon. I bought a small bread roll with the last of my money and ate it while I walked.
When I returned to the office the assistant called me in.
'The editor has read your piece,' he said, 'and he wants to take it. He will pay you ten kroner.'
Ten kroner! It seemed a great sum. I took the money and went out into the street. I stood there a moment, then began to walk. I would eat first.
I went to a restaurant and ate well—the best meal I had had in months. Then I paid for a room for a week. Then I bought some paper and pencils.
I went back to my room and sat at the table. I felt full and warm. I picked up a pencil and thought about what to write next.
But nothing came. The energy of the morning had gone. I sat for a long time, thinking, but my mind was blank. The food had made me drowsy. I lay down on the bed.
I slept until evening. When I woke up I felt rested, but empty again—not hungry for food, but empty in my head. I got up and went out.
I walked through the streets. The lamps were being lit. I walked down towards the harbour.
There was a ship preparing to leave. Sailors were carrying things aboard, and there was an air of departure and movement. I stood and watched.
A mate from the ship came and stood beside me.
'She sails tomorrow,' he said. 'Hamburg first, then further south.'
'Where is she bound?' I asked.
'Cadiz,' he said. 'Maybe Marseilles after that.'
I stood looking at the ship. Something stirred in me—a longing for movement, for new places, for escape from this city that had made its mark upon me.
'Do you need hands?' I asked.
He looked at me.
'Can you work?' he said.
'Yes,' I said. 'I can do what is needed.'
He studied me for a moment.
'Come aboard in the morning,' he said, 'and speak to the captain. Six o'clock. We sail at eight.'
I went back to my room. I had a week's rent paid in advance. I thought about leaving it, about the money wasted. But then I thought about the ship, about Cadiz, about Marseilles.

I sat at my table and wrote a letter to no one—just words on paper, arranging my thoughts. I wrote about Christiania and the months that had passed, about the hunger and the writing, about Ylajali with her dark eyes. I wrote until late.
Then I put the letter away, lay down, and slept.
In the morning I rose early and went to the harbour. The ship was there, smoke already rising from her funnel. I went aboard and found the captain. He was a bluff, red-faced man.
'I want to work for my passage,' I said.
He looked at me for a long time.
'You don't look like much,' he said.
'I am stronger than I look,' I said. And I felt it was true.
He thought about it.
'All right,' he said at last. 'We can use you. It will be hard work.'
'I know,' I said.
I went ashore, back to my room. I took my manuscripts and my notebooks. I left the rest—there was not much. I went back to the ship.

At eight o'clock we moved away from the quay. I stood at the stern and watched Christiania fall away behind us—the wharves, the houses climbing the hills, the fort on the cliff. The strange city that no one leaves before it has set its mark upon him.
It had set its mark upon me. I knew that. Whatever came next, wherever the ship took me, Christiania had marked me and the mark would not come out.
I stood watching until the city disappeared. Then I turned and went forward to find my work.
